channel.title: Posts on mo11om
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on mo11om

item.title: NCTU Plus Frontend
item.author: user@example.com (Hugo Author)
item.description: The Problem The NCTU Plus frontend is a React SPA for NYCU students to browse and post course reviews. Authentication is entirely backend-owned: the Go API handles the NYCU OAuth redirect and sets an httpOnly JWT cookie. The frontend never touches tokens directly — it can only probe session state by calling the API. This shapes every auth-related decision: there is no login form, no token storage, no logout handler on the client side.

item.title: RTX 5090 Ubuntu Server
item.author: user@example.com (Hugo Author)
item.description: The Problem Standing up a GPU compute server sounds straightforward until the RTX 5090 ships with kernel modules that aren’t yet merged upstream. You boot Ubuntu Server, run nvidia-smi, and get nothing — no device, no driver version, just silence. Meanwhile the storage layer has its own complexity: you want OS resilience on a fast ext4 RAID 10, but service data benefits from transparent compression that ext4 can’t offer. Two filesystems, two RAID arrays, one installation pass, and a GPU that may not cooperate until you know exactly where to look in dmesg.

item.title: DNS Server
item.author: user@example.com (Hugo Author)
item.description: The Problem Most networking tutorials treat DNS as a black box: call getaddrinfo(), receive an IP. This project goes the other direction — the server is the resolver. It receives raw DNS queries over UDP, parses binary wire format by hand, looks up records in zone files, and only calls upstream when it can’t answer authoritatively. The engineering challenge is binary protocol implementation: DNS packets have no delimiters, use compressed label encoding, and require byte-order discipline at every field boundary.

item.title: IRC Daemon
item.author: user@example.com (Hugo Author)
item.description: The Problem The naive approach to a multi-client server is one thread per connection: accept a client, spawn a thread, read in a loop. This works up to a few hundred clients, then collapses under thread scheduling overhead and per-thread stack allocation. The alternative is I/O multiplexing: one thread, one poll() call, many file descriptors. The operating system tells you which sockets are ready to read. You service them in sequence.

item.title: LuminaLocal
item.author: user@example.com (Hugo Author)
item.description: The Problem Sending candidate resumes to a cloud LLM API creates two hard constraints that most hiring pipelines ignore: PII leaves your perimeter, and cost scales linearly with batch size. At 500 resumes per hiring cycle, even a compact GPT-4o prompt accumulates thousands of dollars in token spend — before accounting for revision loops. The alternative is local inference. But local models produce unstructured output, hallucinate missing skills, and have no built-in retry logic.

item.title: NCTU Plus API
item.author: user@example.com (Hugo Author)
item.description: The Problema Building an authenticated web platform is straightforward when you own the identity system. NCTU Plus had a harder constraint: authentication must go through NYCU’s OAuth2 provider. The university controls user accounts, session validity, and profile data. Your API is a downstream consumer — you receive an authorization code, exchange it for a profile, and then issue your own session tokens. This creates a three-party trust chain: browser → NYCU OAuth → your API.
